text: Scientific integrity is often seen as an essential ingredient in what has come to be referred to as “evidence-based policy-making”, especially during global emergencies. Whether managing an infectious pandemic, a global financial crisis or climate change there is often an immediate window in which expert advice from scientists and their organisations can have a material impact and where the populations to be advised may be receptive to interventions in the emergency phase of a crisis. Indeed there has been an increasing realisation that the epistemic authority of science may be under threat; but there appears to have been no systematic assessment of what global citizens think about the role of scientists or the extent to which there has been a crisis of trust at a global level. Our paper, drawing on a large pre-registered survey of 71,922 individuals in 68 countries provides a direct assessment of levels of trust across countries. We find that overall citizens in most countries trust scientists and also think that scientists should play more active roles in society and in politics. However there is considerable heterogeneity in trust both at the level of the individual and of countries; and we examine the determinants and implications of trust and consider both a “crisis of trust” narrative and an alternative “harm from the mean” account where while a global “crisis of trust” may not have occurred trust in scientists among even in otherwise trusting populations can be too low to ensure that there is an adequate transmission of scientific-based information to national policymakers to affect policy choice, which is a critical component of the “social contract” between science, the scientists and the citizenry of a country.
headline: Fully Human Written
